MongoDB Connection-Churn Benchmark Report

Generated 2026-06-16 05:33:33 UTC · 3 run(s) loaded.

Starting-state disclosure (Model A, warm cache, cold connections)

Results reflect a warm data cache (untimed pre-read, no retained connections) and a cold connection state under Model A (immutable input). Each Task opens a brand-new connection and closes it (no pooling/reuse between requests). An internal driver pool object may exist, but no pooling/reuse occurs between requests.

Mongo-VM caveat

For mongo-vm, two real-world effects are not exercised and may make production behavior worse than these steady-warm numbers: (a) post-load cold start — the first wave of Tasks right after the daily load hits a partially cold calc_input; and (b) mid-day input updates (Model B append-only / Model C mutable-in-place). Managed services are effectively always warm, so the warm-cache comparison is fair for most of the day but does not capture Mongo's daily cold edge.

No pass/fail thresholds — this is a comparison study. Prioritize p99 (with p95/p99.9) over the average. This benchmark does not represent typical long-lived connection-pool application performance.

Three-way comparison

TargetScenarioTasks (ok/fail)Success %Cycle p99 (ms)Cycle p99.9 (ms)ThrottlingPort exhaustionNo-reuse
cosmos-ruBoth139,629/196,91741.49183230.7187499.4110,2760yes
documentdbBoth480,440/25,64694.9351248.855010.81850yes
mongo-vmBoth464,519/58,56688.8024002.829553.14530yes

Churn-resilience verdict

On the loaded runs, documentdb looks most resilient to connection churn (success 94.93%, cycle p99 51248.8 ms). Weigh this against the Mongo-VM caveat (cold-start / mid-day updates not modeled) and any Cosmos RU throttling or client-side port exhaustion noted per target. This is guidance, not a pass/fail.

Preflight / server configuration (§6.3 check 6)
Preflight artifactnot found in input (run preflight to record server config & index policy)
calc_input ReqId indexNON-UNIQUE
Index uniqueness divergencecosmos-ru ReqId index is non-unique (platform constraint). ReqId == _id verified on 1,000 sampled docs; _id is system-unique on all backends, so distinct ReqId == distinct _id == document count even where the ReqId index is non-unique.

Connection-reuse verification

No-reuse confirmed: YES (created 249,961, closed 249,961, ready 249,957 vs 336,546 Tasks; created/Task 0.743). tasks=336546 (successful=139629), created=249961, ready=249957, closed=249961, checkedOut=861820. No-reuse holds when created>=successfulTasks (one fresh connection per completed Task) and created≈closed (no leak). created<successfulTasks is reuse (suspectedReuseEvents=0); created<totalTasks here is 86585 Tasks that failed at server-selection BEFORE opening a connection, not reuse. Per-Task pool check-outs (≈4×tasks) are normal within a Task.

An internal driver pool object may exist, but no pooling/reuse occurs between requests.
